trajectory_summary: The call's energy built — but Greg built it, not Brandon. Greg arrived warm, disclosed progressively, proposed the site visit himself, and self-closed into a conditional commitment. Brandon executed procedure without adapting to the customer in front of him: he ran a standard bill-collection path at a customer who had just moved in, pitched savings to a customer who said he already understood savings, and bridged to the specialist without acknowledging any of the specific details Greg had shared. The appointment is real, but it was earned by Greg's motivation, not by Brandon's craft.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the call ends with a tentative appointment for April 22 at 1:00 PM. The correct outcome tag is 'tentative_appointment.' The appointment is structurally fragile: it was booked without confirming who else is on the title, without a computer/no-driving anchor, without an 'is that fair' verbal handshake, and with Greg's explicit conditional ('if it's cost effective') left unaddressed. The sit is at risk of no-show or disqualification at the closer stage.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I do have someone in the area who can meet with you. As soon as tomorrow, what day would work best for you and you have to take a look at evaluation?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Brandon books the appointment without confirming who else is on the title, without a computer/no-driving anchor, without an 'is that fair' handshake, and without a text follow-up promise. Greg's repeated use of 'we' — including 'when we come back' at 09:54 — signals a co-decision-maker who is unidentified and not included in the appointment. These are the two highest-leverage structural gaps for show rate and close rate on this specific call.

key_moments.quote: the property has two structures, and on the second structure, we have a roof a perfect roof to put a solar energy system on because it's it's a pretty large space, you know, covering probably around a thousand square feet, and it's, it's also all perfectly south facing.
key_moments.stewart_read: Greg volunteers a high-value qualifier unprompted: a second structure with a large, south-facing roof he's already identified as ideal for solar. This is not a casual aside — it's Greg doing the rep's qualification work for him. Brandon's response is to ask about the referral program, which is procedurally reasonable but misses the moment entirely. The two-structure detail resurfaces at 08:43 when Greg mentions a HERS score for one structure, confirming this is a material lead characteristic, not a throwaway comment.

key_moments.quote: we'd also would wanna know on the, you know, the the the cost the cost sharing program that if there's any, like, you know, monies available to apply for to, to offset some of the costs of of installation as well as, you know, low interest loan programs and things like that or leasing options.
key_moments.stewart_read: Greg is asking about financing structures — closer-tier content. Brandon's response ('that's what we will go over with you') is a procedurally correct deferral, but it's delivered without any acknowledgment of Greg's sophistication. The strategic read: Brandon is correctly holding scope. The reactive read: the deferral is flat and generic, which makes it feel like Brandon doesn't know the answer rather than that he's appropriately routing the question. The distinction matters for Greg, who has already signaled he's done his homework.

key_moments.quote: So the idea would be if we have the solar, you know, we could we could then it doesn't really matter if it's less efficient because we have the solar system to provide the energy.
key_moments.stewart_read: Greg has just done the objection inversion himself — he's reframed his inefficient heating system from a liability into a reason solar makes even more sense. This is a flip opportunity the rep walked past entirely. Brandon's next line (06:09) is 'Right. Okay. So who do you have for the utility company?' — a procedural pivot that ignores the logic Greg just handed him. The correct move is to land on Greg's own framing: 'Exactly — and that's actually the argument for sizing the system to cover the inefficiency rather than fixing it. That's the kind of math the specialist will run for you.'

key_moments.quote: we wanna go solar to be more renewable than we were. We also wanna lower our carbon footprint. There's there's a number of reasons. Right? We wanna save some money, of course, but we also, in addition to saving money, we're also, you know, wanna be more renewable in the way we're living.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the deepest motivation disclosure on the call — Greg is volunteering his full value stack: environmental values, cost strategy, and a specific rationale for solar over HVAC repair. The verify.interest_in_solar item is designed to surface exactly this. Brandon never asked for it; Greg offered it anyway. The coaching gap is not that Brandon failed to extract it — it's that he did nothing with it. There is no acknowledgment of the environmental motivation, no tie-back to it in the button-up, and no mention of it in the text follow-up promise (which was never made). Per text_after_call, the tieback to interests is a required element of the post-call text.
